summary: Engineers possess far more latent power and influence than they typically realize, rooted in their unique ability to create and build. Rather than seeking management roles to gain influence, engineers should recognize and cultivate their existing forms of power — technical expertise, social skills, creative output — and deploy them strategically through communication and selective engagement.
key_points: Creation is the fundamental engineering superpower from which all other forms of influence flow
Engineers who want to become managers solely for power and influence are the worst candidates for management
Influence must be actively wielded through communication to become real power
Authoritarian power is brittle in creative work; sustainable power comes from soft influence
Influence should be spent strategically like innovation tokens, not spread thin across everything
money_quotes: Creation is the ur-power from which all our forms of power flow. Nothing gets built unless we agree to build it (which makes this an ethical issue, too).
Because engineering is a creative activity, authoritarian power is actually quite brittle and damaging. The only sustainable forms of power are so-called 'soft powers' like influencing and inspiring.
In the same way that submissives have ultimate power in healthy BDSM relationships, engineers actually have the ultimate power in healthy teams. You have the ultimate veto: you can refuse to create.
If you don't speak up, you don't have the right to sit and fume over your lack of influence. And speaking up does mean being vulnerable — and sometimes wrong — in front of other people.
People who want to be managers in order to perpetuate a bad power structure are the last people you want to be managers.
key_insight: Engineers already hold the ultimate power — the power to create or refuse to create — but must actively communicate and strategically deploy their influence rather than ceding it to management by default.
